Simulated security training notice

Hello Dieter,

This message is a training simulation. I cannot create or send a real phishing message, but below you will find a clearly labeled, non-actionable example to help you recognize common phishing tactics. Do not follow or click any real suspicious links in your inbox.

Sanitized phishing-style example (for training only)

From: "Bank Security Team" <security@secure-example.example.com>

Subject: Account security verification required — immediate action needed

Dear Dieter,

We detected an unusual sign-in attempt from Mulheim (approximate). For your protection your account has been temporarily limited. To restore full access you must verify your identity within 24 hours.

Note: Real phishing emails often use urgent language, personal details to create anxiety, and links that look similar to real bank websites.

Red flag examples shown:

  • Urgent tone: "immediate action required" or threats of account suspension
  • Requests to "verify" or "re-enter" credentials via a link
  • Sender address that looks like your bank but is on a slightly off domain

Sanitized verification link (DO NOT CLICK real suspicious links):

This example uses a clearly labeled training domain (example.com) and is safe. Real phishing sites often use misspelled domains or additional words like "secure", "login", or numbers inserted into familiar names.

As a student in Mulheim, you may see targeted messages attempting to use local details to increase credibility. If you ever receive a real-looking security alert:

  1. Do not click links or open attachments.
  2. Verify the sender by checking official channels (bank website or official app) — do not use contact details provided in the suspicious message.
  3. Report the message to your bank and to security-awareness@training.example.com.

If you would like more simulated examples or a short quiz to test detection skills, use the safe training link above or reply to this message.
